title: SSRF via attacker-controlled attachment URL in WXR import file
entry_point: Tools > Import > WordPress (Step 2 with 'Download and import file attachments' checkbox enabled)
root_cause: The <wp:attachment_url> element in a WXR file is parsed into $post['attachment_url'] without any sanitization or allowlist validation. This value is used directly as the URL for wp_safe_remote_get() in fetch_remote_file(). While wp_safe_remote_get() calls WP_HTTP::block_request() to block RFC1918 private ranges (10/8, 172.16/12, 192.168/16) and loopback (127/8), it does NOT block 169.254.0.0/16 (link-local / APIPA). This gap allows SSRF to cloud Instance Metadata Service (IMDS) endpoints which universally reside at 169.254.169.254 across AWS, Azure, GCP, and Oracle Cloud.
taint_flow: HTTP GET fires to 169.254.169.254 — not blocked by block_request()

exploitation.requirement: Authenticated administrator account; admin must check 'Download and import file attachments' in the import wizard UI
exploitation.attack_scenario: Attacker social-engineers admin into importing a malicious WXR file (e.g., disguised as a site migration export, shared via a compromised plugin/theme marketplace, sent via phishing). The file contains attachment posts with URLs pointing to cloud IMDS or internal services.
steps: 1. Craft a WXR file containing an attachment post with <wp:attachment_url>http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/</wp:attachment_url>
2. Deliver WXR to admin (social engineering, compromised export source, etc.)
3. Admin navigates to Tools > Import > WordPress, uploads WXR file
4. On Step 2, admin checks 'Download and import file attachments' (standard workflow for migrating sites with media)
6. On AWS EC2 with IMDSv1: IAM credential JSON is streamed to a temp file (/tmp/wordpress-importer-*.tmp)
7. wp_check_filetype() rejects the JSON response (not a recognized media type); temp file is deleted
8. Attacker cannot directly read the response body through the import flow

9. However: (a) 300-second timeout creates a detectable timing difference vs. unreachable host; (b) error messages may leak response characteristics; (c) with a collaborating external server, DNS rebinding can be used to exfiltrate response body
exploitation.notes: IMDSv2 (AWS) requires a PUT request with X-aws-ec2-metadata-token-ttl-seconds header to obtain a session token first; a plain GET to 169.254.169.254 will return a 401, confirming SSRF but not yielding credentials. IMDSv1 (legacy, still common) responds directly to GET requests. Azure IMDS requires Metadata: true header. GCP IMDS requires Metadata-Flavor: Google header. WordPress does not add these headers, so credential theft is IMDS-version dependent. The SSRF probe itself (for service enumeration/timing) works regardless of IMDS version.

also_exploitable_via: The <guid> element is used as fallback if <wp:attachment_url> is absent (line 875: $remote_url = ! empty($post['attachment_url']) ? $post['attachment_url'] : $post['guid']). The <guid> element is also fully attacker-controlled in the WXR file.
base_url_amplification: The <wp:base_site_url> element populates $this->base_url (line ~84 of class-wp-import.php). If an attachment has a relative URL (matching /^/[\w\W]+$/), the plugin prepends $this->base_url (line 1303-1305). An attacker can set base_site_url to http://169.254.169.254 and use a relative attachment path like /latest/meta-data/ to achieve the same SSRF.
impact: Server-Side Request Forgery enabling: (1) Cloud IMDS probing for metadata and credentials (169.254.169.254, not blocked); (2) Internal network service enumeration via timing side-channels; (3) Potential credential theft on cloud instances with IMDSv1 enabled (if response exfiltration channel is established); (4) Bypass of network-level access controls that block external access to internal services.

remediation.primary: Validate attachment URLs against an allowlist of trusted domains before fetching, or implement a user-configurable allowlist of permitted source domains.
remediation.secondary: Extend WP_HTTP::block_request() (or apply a pre_http_request filter in the plugin) to also block 169.254.0.0/16 link-local addresses. The fix: if the resolved IP falls in 169.254.0.0/16, block the request.
